stem: You are the psychiatry consultant assessing a 32-year-old solicitor self-referred for poor focus and procrastination that has worsened with increasing job demands. School reports from her primary school show comments about daydreaming, organisation difficulties, and underachievement relative to expected ability. She has tried mindfulness and study planners without sustained improvement. She has no cardiac history or family sudden death history.

data_table.Examination: Mental state shows good rapport, normal mood, no anxiety disorder identified on screening tools, no substance use
data_table.Investigations: WURS (childhood retrospective) and DIVA-5 (adult interview) both meet ADHD criteria, ECG normal, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan after sharing the diagnosis?

options.text: Trial methylphenidate immediate release with structured titration, ECG and BP monitoring, and adjunctive psychological support and workplace accommodations
options.rationale: Stimulants (methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine) are first-line for adult ADHD with structured titration and cardiovascular monitoring per RANZCP. (Source: RANZCP ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Commence atomoxetine as first-line therapy to avoid the abuse potential associated with stimulant medication in a professional patient with longstanding symptoms
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is second-line for adult ADHD; stimulants have greater effect size and are first-line. (Source: RANZCP ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Recommend ongoing cognitive behavioural therapy alone without pharmacotherapy given the patient is functional and the symptoms are likely related to occupational stress
options.rationale: Diagnosis meets criteria and prior non-pharmacological strategies have been tried; pharmacotherapy is indicated. (Source: RANZCP ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Trial sertraline 50 mg daily for presumed inattention secondary to subthreshold anxiety symptoms identified on the clinical interview at the initial consultation
options.rationale: Anxiety screening was negative and SSRIs do not treat core ADHD symptoms. (Source: RANZCP ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for sleep study to exclude obstructive sleep apnoea before considering pharmacotherapy or further psychological intervention for the presenting symptoms
options.rationale: BMI 22 and no snoring or fatigue features make OSA unlikely; deferring treatment is not appropriate. (Source: RANZCP ADHD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD requires confirmation of childhood onset (before age 12) and symptoms across two settings. DIVA-5 plus collateral or school records are standard. Pharmacotherapy is first-line for moderate to severe symptoms; methylphenidate and lisdexamfetamine have largest effect sizes. ECG and BP monitoring at baseline and during titration. Atomoxetine and guanfacine are second-line. Psychological strategies augment but do not replace pharmacotherapy in moderate to severe cases.
key_points: Childhood onset before 12 years required for adult diagnosis
Methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine first-line; atomoxetine second-line
ECG and BP monitoring at baseline and during dose escalation
explanation.pearls: Stimulants are PBS streamlined authority for adult ADHD when criteria met and require an accredited prescriber in some states; check state-specific S8 prescribing requirements.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

stem: You are the consult-liaison psychiatry registrar called to a general medical ward for a 64-year-old man admitted three days ago with severe depression who has now become mute and immobile. He has not eaten for 24 hours and his fluid intake is minimal. He keeps the position his arm is moved into for prolonged periods. He has no fever and is otherwise haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, eyes open, waxy flexibility with sustained arm positioning, mild rigidity, no clonus, GCS technically 11 (E4 V1 M6) but neurologically intact when assessed indirectly
data_table.Investigations: CK 320 (slightly raised), FBC, UEC, LFTs, calcium, glucose, urine drug screen all unremarkable, CT brain normal
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 200 mg started three weeks ago for severe MDD

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous fluids and nutritional support while awaiting routine review by the inpatient psychiatry team at the start of the following week
options.rationale: Waiting risks malignant catatonia and complications; lorazepam challenge should not be delayed. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

options.text: Commence intravenous haloperidol 5 mg followed by repeat dosing every twenty minutes until the patient demonstrates clear response to behavioural redirection on the ward
options.rationale: Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and risk precipitating neuroleptic malignant syndrome; they are contraindicated as first-line. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

options.text: Administer intravenous lorazepam 2 mg as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge with formal reassessment for catatonia response within ten minutes
options.rationale: Lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg IV) is the standard diagnostic and therapeutic intervention; response within 10 minutes confirms catatonia and guides ongoing therapy. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

options.text: Refer urgently for ECT as primary treatment without trialling pharmacotherapy first given the severe presentation and degree of functional incapacity observed on the ward
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective and may be needed; however, lorazepam challenge is the first-line initial step and may be sufficient on its own. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

options.text: Increase sertraline to 300 mg daily and add olanzapine 10 mg nightly to augment the antidepressant response while continuing observation on the medical ward
options.rationale: SSRI increase does not treat catatonia rapidly; olanzapine risks worsening catatonia and precipitating NMS. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is identified by Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale features including mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, posturing, staring, and negativism. Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV is both diagnostic (response within 10 minutes) and therapeutic, with up to 80% response rates. ECT is the next step if lorazepam fails or if malignant catatonia features (fever, autonomic instability, rigidity, raised CK) develop. Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and may precipitate NMS.
key_points: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV is first-line diagnostic and therapeutic
Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and risk NMS
ECT is highly effective for refractory or malignant catatonia
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (fever, autonomic instability, raised CK) shares features with NMS; treatment is the same with lorazepam, supportive care, and early ECT if no rapid response.

stem: You are the general medicine RMO assessing a 58-year-old woman presenting with three days of left calf swelling and tenderness. She returned from a 14-hour flight a week ago. She has hypertension on perindopril, no prior VTE, and no active cancer. Examination reveals a swollen tender left calf with 3 cm circumference difference compared to the right and pitting oedema confined to the symptomatic leg.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82, HR 84, RR 16, SpO2 98% room air, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Left calf swollen with 3 cm circumference difference at 10 cm below tibial tuberosity, tender along deep venous distribution, pitting oedema confined to symptomatic leg, no superficial varicosities

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Arrange an MRI venogram of the lower limb to fully characterise venous involvement and exclude alternative diagnoses including muscular and lymphatic pathology
options.rationale: MR venography is reserved for specific cases (pelvic veins, IVC); not first-line for typical leg DVT. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: VTE)

options.text: Send a D-dimer level as the first step regardless of pretest probability because a negative result rules out deep vein thrombosis in all patient groups including this one
options.rationale: D-dimer rules out only with low pretest probability; with Wells 3 or more, proceed to imaging directly. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: VTE)

options.text: Arrange CT venography of the lower limb as the most sensitive imaging test for proximal and distal vein involvement before initiating any further intervention
options.rationale: Compression ultrasound is first-line; CT venography is not standard initial imaging for DVT. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: VTE)

options.text: Commence therapeutic anticoagulation with apixaban and arrange ultrasound for confirmation if symptoms have not resolved over the next seven to ten days
options.rationale: Anticoagulation may be commenced empirically if imaging is delayed but diagnosis must still be confirmed promptly; deferring confirmation 7 to 10 days is inappropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: VTE)

options.text: Compression duplex ultrasound of the proximal leg veins given her Wells score makes DVT likely and pretest probability favours imaging over D-dimer
options.rationale: Wells score 3 or more (likely DVT) directs straight to compression ultrasound rather than D-dimer; her score totals 3 (calf swelling, pitting oedema unilateral, recent immobilisation). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: VTE)

explanation.summary: DVT workup uses the Wells score to stratify pretest probability. Low probability (under 2) allows D-dimer rule-out. Likely DVT (2 or more) directs straight to compression ultrasound. Therapeutic guidance favours apixaban or rivaroxaban for proximal DVT without cancer or severe renal impairment. Provoked DVT (long-haul flight, surgery, immobilisation) is treated for 3 months; unprovoked DVT requires extended duration consideration.
key_points: Wells score guides whether to use D-dimer or imaging first
Compression duplex ultrasound is first-line imaging
Apixaban or rivaroxaban first-line for non-cancer DVT; 3 months for provoked
explanation.pearls: Cancer-associated thrombosis: LMWH or DOAC (apixaban, edoxaban) both acceptable per ASH 2021; rivaroxaban has higher bleeding risk in GI cancers.

stem: You are the ED RMO called to a 38-year-old man who developed widespread urticaria, throat tightness, and wheeze 15 minutes after eating prawns at a work lunch. He has previously had mild oral itching with shellfish. There has been no previous severe reaction. The triage nurse has placed IV access and is preparing equipment.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/58, HR 124, RR 26, SpO2 92% room air, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: Generalised urticaria, audible wheeze, mild lip swelling, no stridor, alert

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg as the first intervention for the systemic inflammatory response before any other pharmacological intervention is undertaken
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have slow onset and are adjunctive; they do not replace adrenaline as the time-critical intervention. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg into the anterolateral thigh and position the patient supine while preparing further supportive measures
options.rationale: IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg up to 0.5 mg into anterolateral thigh is first-line for anaphylaxis; delay increases biphasic and fatal reaction risk. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous promethazine 25 mg to manage the urticarial component and reassess the need for adrenaline once the antihistamine has been completed
options.rationale: Antihistamines treat urticaria but not airway or cardiovascular compromise; adrenaline must come first. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 5 mg as the first treatment for the bronchospasm and reassess oxygen requirements before considering further pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Salbutamol may help bronchospasm but does not address upper airway swelling or hypotension; adrenaline IM is first. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline infusion at 0.05 micrograms/kg/min titrated to blood pressure response without prior intramuscular dose of adrenaline at all
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis after multiple IM doses; IM is first-line due to safer pharmacokinetics. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is defined by acute onset (minutes to hours) with respiratory or cardiovascular compromise plus skin or mucosal involvement, or hypotension after known allergen exposure. IM adrenaline (1:1000) 0.01 mg/kg up to 0.5 mg in adults into anterolateral thigh is first-line. Repeat every 5 minutes as needed. Lay patient flat (head up if breathing difficulty), IV fluids for hypotension, salbutamol for persistent wheeze, corticosteroids as adjunct. Observe at least 4 hours.
key_points: IM adrenaline first-line; do not delay for IV access
Lay patient flat; sitting up increases mortality risk in hypotension
Observe 4 hours minimum; biphasic reaction in up to 20%
explanation.pearls: ASCIA action plan emphasises supine positioning; sitting up or standing during anaphylaxis is associated with empty ventricle syndrome and sudden death.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 64-year-old man brought to ED with sudden severe headache, vomiting, and confusion. He has known hypertension and stopped his perindopril two months ago. CT brain shows a small right basal ganglia haemorrhage. His GCS is 14, no focal neurology beyond mild left arm weakness, and he is haemodynamically stable. The stroke registrar is on the way.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 218/124 confirmed on repeated manual measurement, HR 78, RR 16, SpO2 97%, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6), mild left arm weakness 4/5, no neglect, fundi show grade III changes
data_table.Investigations: CT brain: 12 mL right basal ganglia haemorrhage, no IVH, no mass effect; sodium 138, potassium 4.0, creatinine 92
data_table.Medications: Recently stopped perindopril 10 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, aspirin 100 mg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management of his blood pressure?

options.text: Commence intravenous nitroprusside infusion with target systolic blood pressure of 100 mmHg within the first thirty minutes to minimise the risk of further bleeding
options.rationale: Nitroprusside risks cyanide accumulation and can raise ICP; aggressive lowering to 100 risks hypoperfusion. Target is 140 systolic. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Living Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral nifedipine 20 mg immediate release with reassessment every fifteen minutes for further dose titration as needed for ongoing severely elevated blood pressure
options.rationale: Sublingual or oral immediate-release nifedipine causes unpredictable rapid BP drop and is contraindicated in acute stroke. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Living Guidelines)

options.text: Resume oral perindopril at 10 mg daily and arrange admission for monitoring without intravenous antihypertensive therapy given the established mild blood pressure background
options.rationale: Oral perindopril has slow onset and cannot control hypertensive emergency; IV agents are needed. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Living Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous labetalol with a systolic blood pressure target of 140 mmHg within the first hour as per current intracerebral haemorrhage guidance
options.rationale: Intracerebral haemorrhage with systolic over 180 is treated with IV labetalol targeting systolic 140 within 1 hour per INTERACT2 and AHA guidance; smooth controlled lowering reduces haematoma growth. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Living Guidelines)

options.text: Permit permissive hypertension with no antihypertensive therapy in the first six hours to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure and avoid worsening neurological status
options.rationale: Permissive hypertension applies to ischaemic stroke (not for thrombolysis) and not to ICH; ICH benefits from intensive BP lowering. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia Living Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute intracerebral haemorrhage with systolic BP over 180 mmHg is managed by smooth controlled BP lowering targeting systolic 140 within 1 hour per INTERACT2. Labetalol is first-line in Australia; alternatives include glyceryl trinitrate or hydralazine. Avoid sublingual nifedipine and nitroprusside. Reverse anticoagulation, neurosurgical consult, and care in stroke unit improve outcomes. Ischaemic stroke uses different targets (permissive unless thrombolysis or over 220/120).
key_points: ICH systolic target 140 within 1 hour per INTERACT2
Ischaemic stroke: permissive hypertension unless over 220/120 or pre-thrombolysis (under 185/110)
Avoid sublingual nifedipine and nitroprusside in acute stroke
explanation.pearls: INTERACT2 showed intensive BP lowering (target 140 systolic) in ICH did not reduce mortality but improved functional outcomes; ATACH-II did not replicate this benefit, but Australian Living Guidelines retain the 140 target.
